name: CAIRN Protocol
description:
A cairn is a stack of stones left by travelers to mark the path - so the next traveler knows where to go, and where not to. Each cairn is left by one traveler but read by every traveler who comes after. No traveler owns the cairn network. Every traveler benefits from it.

CAIRN applies this ancient pattern to autonomous agents. When an agent accepts a task, it emits heartbeats to prove liveness and writes checkpoints after each subtask - each checkpoint a stone placed on the trail. If the agent fails (rate limit, crash, budget exceeded), the protocol detects it automatically through missed heartbeats. No human required. No trusted keeper. Anyone can trigger enforcement.

The protocol classifies the failure, computes a recovery score, and if recovery is viable, assigns a fallback agent from the Olas Mech Marketplace. The fallback reads the checkpoint history and resumes from the last verified state - not from zero. The original agent gets paid for the checkpoints they completed. The fallback gets paid for finishing. Fair settlement, enforced by escrow.

Every failure and every resolution writes an execution record to a shared intelligence layer. Before starting any task, agents query this layer: which APIs rate-limit at 2am, which task types exceed budgets, which agents have the highest success rates for this exact operation. The ecosystem navigates by accumulated intelligence, not blind optimism.

This is the network effect that makes CAIRN compound: the more agents integrate, the richer the intelligence layer becomes. Competitors can copy the code. They cannot copy the history.

Six smart contracts on Base Sepolia: CairnCore (6-state machine), RecoveryRouter (failure classification), FallbackPool (agent selection), ArbiterRegistry (disputes), CairnGovernance (parameters), CairnTaskMVP (simplified entry point). 302 Foundry tests. 98.95% coverage. Integrates ERC-8183 escrow hooks, ERC-8004 agent identity, ERC-7710 delegation, and Olas Mech Marketplace with 600+ fallback agents.
problemStatement: Agent workflows have compounding failure rates. At 85% success per action, a 10-step workflow completes only 20% of the time. When failures happen today: work restarts from zero, escrow locks for hours or days, and human intervention is required to resolve ambiguous states. The same failure repeats across the ecosystem because nothing is shared, nothing is learned. CAIRN changes this. It preserves work via on-chain checkpoints, automatically assigns fallback agents when heartbeats timeout, and settles escrow proportionally to verified progress. Each checkpoint is a cairn - a marker left by one agent for the next, so no work is ever lost and every failure teaches the network.
conversationLog: Claude-Opus-4.5 via claude-code, 12 sessions, 7 days, zero human code commits. Contracts (Sessions 1-4): CairnCore, CairnTaskMVP, RecoveryRouter, FallbackPool, ArbiterRegistry, CairnGovernance deployed to Base Sepolia. 302 tests at 98.95% coverage. SDK (Sessions 5-7): Python CairnClient, CairnAgent, CheckpointStore with async/await and Merkle batching. 126 tests. Frontend (Sessions 8-10): Next.js 14 with wagmi event watching for real-time state updates. Integration (Sessions 11-12): The Graph subgraph, off-chain event listener, Bonfires knowledge graph connection. Technical: UUPS proxies, CEI pattern, ReentrancyGuard, custom errors, Merkle batching for 89-99% checkpoint gas reduction.
